# Model Setup {#sec-setup}

## Environment

There are two players: labor ($L$) and management ($M$). They compete over which side's preferred policy is implemented. The outcome is winner-take-all: the winner receives its own prize value, while the loser receives 0. Contest technology follows \citet{tullock1980}; endogenous timing follows \citet{hamilton1990} and \citet{fu2006}.

::: {#def-prizes}
## Asymmetric Prize Values

If labor wins, labor obtains $V_L>0$ and management obtains 0. If management wins, management obtains $V_M\equiv V>0$ and labor obtains 0. Prize values need not be symmetric.
:::

## Information Structure

Before timing or effort choices, labor privately observes its own prize value $V_L$. Management knows only the distribution of $V_L$. We assume $V_L$ has support set $[\mu-\delta,\mu+\delta]$, with $\mu>\delta>0$, so that $\E[V_L]=\mu$.

Define

$$
u_\ell \equiv \sqrt{\mu-\delta}, \qquad
u_H \equiv \sqrt{\mu+\delta}.
$$ {#eq-u-types}

We use two baseline distributions.

::: {#def-binary}
### Binary Distribution {#sec-distributions}

$$
V_L \in \{V_\ell,V_H\}=\{\mu-\delta,\mu+\delta\},
$$

with probability $1/2$ on each type. Hence $\Var(V_L)=\delta^2$.
:::

::: {#def-uniform}
### Uniform Distribution

$$
V_L \sim U[\mu-\delta,\mu+\delta],
$$

with density $f(v)=1/(2\delta)$. Hence $\Var(V_L)=\delta^2/3$.
:::

Both distributions share the same mean $\mu$ and support set $[\mu-\delta,\mu+\delta]$, but differ in variance. Equilibrium expressions depend on two key moments:

$$
\kappa \equiv \E\!\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{V_L}}\right],
\qquad
\nu \equiv \E\!\left[\sqrt{V_L}\right].
$$ {#eq-moments}

::: {#prp-moments}
### Moment Comparison

The key moments are:

|  | Binary | Uniform |
|---|---|---|
| $\Var(V_L)$ | $\delta^2$ | $\delta^2/3$ |
| $\kappa$ | $\dfrac{1}{2u_\ell}+\dfrac{1}{2u_H}$ | $\dfrac{u_H-u_\ell}{\delta}$ |
| $\nu$ | $\dfrac{u_\ell+u_H}{2}$ | $\dfrac{u_H^3-u_\ell^3}{3\delta}$ |

: Key moments under binary and uniform distributions {#tbl-moments}
:::

::: {.proof}
For the binary case, moments follow directly from definition. For the uniform case:

$$
\kappa^U
=\frac{1}{2\delta}\int_{\mu-\delta}^{\mu+\delta} v^{-1/2}\,dv
=\frac{[2\sqrt v]_{\mu-\delta}^{\mu+\delta}}{2\delta}
=\frac{u_H-u_\ell}{\delta},
$$

$$
\nu^U
=\frac{1}{2\delta}\int_{\mu-\delta}^{\mu+\delta} v^{1/2}\,dv
=\frac{[\tfrac{2}{3}v^{3/2}]_{\mu-\delta}^{\mu+\delta}}{2\delta}
=\frac{u_H^3-u_\ell^3}{3\delta}.
$$
:::

## Contest Payoffs and Best Responses

The Tullock success function is $p_i=x_i/(x_L+x_M)$. Net expected payoffs are

$$
U_L(x_L,x_M;V_L)=\frac{x_L}{x_L+x_M}V_L-x_L,
$$ {#eq-ul}

$$
U_M(x_L,x_M;V)=\frac{x_M}{x_L+x_M}V-x_M.
$$ {#eq-um}

First-order conditions imply best responses:

$$
x_L^*(x_M;V_L)=\sqrt{V_Lx_M}-x_M,
$$ {#eq-brl}

$$
x_M^*(x_L;V)=\sqrt{Vx_L}-x_L.
$$ {#eq-brm}

Management's best response @eq-brm depends only on observed effort $x_L$ and known value $V$. Beliefs about labor types do not directly enter the second-stage best response. This is central for the separation-versus-pooling result later.
